I switched to Signal after getting hacked at Hangouts that a friend talked me into .
I’m so glad I did my research because that was a year ago and I have not been disappointed.  Chatting on the phone is so clear, FaceTime is so clear even overseas, I like the quick voice messages you can leave.  It’s easy to send 5 photos at a time to share.  
For the person upset about not saving history, etc ... go back to messenger or hangouts.  Different people want different things, and I for one prefer not to have anyone spying or breaking into my conversations.  It’s irrelevant that what you are talking about isn’t top secret ... it’s personal and not anyone’s business except whom I’m talking to.  
As for stickers ... geez, I’m not juvenile.  I dint need silly stickers and gifs.  Once again, go use another app that provides that stuff.  Personally I love the simple cleanliness of the app.  If you want to save a photo sent you or a piece if conversation containing something you wish to keep, screen shot it.  Crop the picture for for photos and stick the info you saved to a file folder or notes.  
I’d give it ten stars if I could.  My only wish is that it only gets better and not worse, like so many apps do after they have been out there for so long.  I hope the developers maintain their integrity and keep this app private

Finally came in for lunch at Deadhorse Hill, after hearing tons of awesome reviews. I sat at the bar prior to the lunch rush coming in (patrons began arriving and filling up the seats during my time here). 

The server was so sweet. We spoke at lengths about the restaurant, the food, and the extensive drink menu. It is amazing how many different bottles of alcohol they have available here.

I ordered the Grilled Cheese with local Swiss and cheddar cheeses, caramelized onions and bacon on crusty sourdough bread. And to drink, I decided a great pairing would be their mimosa with sparkling wine and fresh pressed juices.

The drink came quickly and was the perfect mix of wine and juice. Yummy!

The meal arrived not long after. The sandwich was steaming hot. The cheese was ooey and gooey - just the way it should be in a grilled cheese. I wasn't expecting the home made potato chips, as they were not specified on the menu. They were crispy and seasoned just right.

The grilled cheese sandwich was probably one of the best I have ever had. When the server came to remove my plate (a different one than the one who took my order), she suggested I order it with tomatoes next time to make it even more juicy.

All and all, the food was top notch, the staff were the best I have ever encountered in a restaurant. No matter how busy they got, I never felt neglected. I look forward to coming back for dinner some night soon.

You can report them, but they keep coming back (and I suspect it’s really just an ad - by clicking report, you’re making sure Tinder gets paid for the product placement hit.)
2) Slightly less bviously fake profiles of varying age/attractiveness - one photo, no info.
3) Dubious profiles of somewhat attractive women who will talk to you for up to one day and then stop responding in the middle of a conversation.  I used to think maybe it was me being boring; but now I don’t think so.  I think maybe it’s all one dude in a basement in Utah, and he only has time for so many replies per person.
4) Supposed polyamorous couples looking for cute women to join them (yet they always show up in MY queue.) Why is the guy in these couples ALWAYS white, short, chubby, and bearded?  I’m pretty sure his name is always Craig. Or David. You can just tell.
5) Meth or “People of Wal-Mart” quality profiles. I’m not sure if these are real or not; I mean, some of them are pretty entertaining in a train-wreck sort of way.  There’s one that keeps popping up under different names (same photos) and I laugh and laugh and laugh when I see it because I can literally imagine the sound effects this person makes IRL.

Long story short: there is no middle ground - normal women interested in meeting new people and taking it from there. I suspect there are really zero REAL WOMEN on this app at all. And I also suspect it’s by design:

Draw men in with what amounts to soft-core pornography profiles.
Fill out the app with tons of basic one photo/no info profiles so it seems like there are a lot of people on there (who aren’t quite so obviously fake.)
Every once in a while, throw a match your way and talk for a half a day, just so you don’t believe the app is all complete BS.  

Think about it - how do they make money off you if they actually connect you with someone to hang out with, go on dates with (or just hook up, if that’s what you’re looking for) or your special someone? They don’t. They make money by sneaking ads in there disguised as attractive women and keeping you just interested enough to keep the app installed - while also spying on your location.

So, honestly, if you’re a halfway decent human being with any sort of standards, this and ALL the other dating apps (including OKC, POF, Bumble, Match, and CMB) are a complete waste of time. Time that could be spent going out and living a real and well-rounded life where you actually do fun activities and you know you’re talking to a real person (and when the conversation ends, it’s because it has resolved itself naturally.)

Do NOT waste your time on this app.  Shame on whoever is responsible for making it WORSE than a complete waste of time.

I loved this app for it’s signature covert ‘disguise’ feature it use to provide. It use to give you options for how you wanted the app’s preface to look or function, such as a simple and obvious password request when opening (like it currently has), or by looking like a functioning calculator that actually did simple computations but would open up into your secret vault when the correct passcode was entered into the calculator. 

Now it’s preface looks like every other hidden vault app out there. Anyone who taps it will know immediately that there is something behind the app to try to pry into.

Even better, why would anyone who doesn’t own your phone feel a need to delete a calculator? But an obvious, passcode protected ‘secret’ vault? Any jealous or untrusting partner might get frustrated and just maliciously delete the app. 

Everything...gone. 

With the calculator feature, it looked like a boring calculator with a funny name...it would have likely been ignored. 

It obviously wouldn’t have taken much for someone to go investigating the app on the App Store for more info, but who looks up background info on a minimalistic calculator app? 

I get that a parallel vault can now be created as a diversion from the real one should the owner of the app be requested to open it, but that’s beside the point. It felt kind of cool having a dual function app that masqueraded as something else, but functioned perfectly well as a calculator as well as a hidden vault. 

Of course, beyond that, my biggest complaint before this change was that the Kyms Calc app would show in settings under “Apps that have requested access to photos album” if you even once attempted to transfer media from your photos app over to the Kyms Calc app. The obvious question here for snoopers to ask would have been “Why does a calculator need to access a photo album...ever?!”

Even worse, If you had sizeable files, like a movie, etc, it would show up under the listing of apps on your iPhone under the iPhone Storage section in settings and rank higher and higher up the list (front and center) the more space it took up. If you had anything in there that you didn’t feel like showing someone this could cause a problem. All someone would have to do would be to ask why a lowly, minimalist calculator takes up more than its’ App Store advertised 60.5 megs. A movie of about 1 gig or so, which is a modest sized movie, would push the app up to the top as one of your more storage-greedy apps. 

So, if there’s a silver lining to all of this, at least now someone won’t be tricked into thinking it’s something else only to discover in the iPhone Storage section and the Photo Album Requests section that it’s more than meets the eye. You won’t have to awkwardly explain to someone, red-handed, that it’s something masquerading as something else. 

But remember, even now...if you create a dummy parallel account, and hardly put anything into it, and someone goes into the iPhone Storage section, they will still see that the app is much bigger than it should be. Anyone aspiring to be Nancy Drew or The Hardy Boys can make short detective work of that facade in no time by looking it up in the App Store. 

A lowly calculator likely would not have triggered that scrutiny at face value. 

But even if someone doesn’t go into the iphone storage section, simply clicking on the app itself to be instantly confronted by a password request is likely to illicit detective work, even if just out of curiosity. 

And if their intellect is not to be questioned, the first places they’re going to look for more info is on the App Store or Internet. 

And that’s when they find out the app offers a parallel dummy account to stave off nosey people while attempting to let the owner seem honest and open. 

Busted!

At any rate, all in all, I still like the app for its vault features, but I fell in love with it initially over all others because it was a secret app within a separate, but functional app. 

No longer.

Was kinda spy-worthy in a way. Gave it real appeal & set it apart from other obvious secret (NOT so secret) vaults. 

Will upgrade rating if the Calc facade or something better returns to the app to give it the secret squirrel feel again. Until then, I will keep looking for a quality replacement that possesses what this app lost. 

And you should too.

I also understand there was a case involving children/teens using such an app to trick their parents w/it’s unassuming calculator preface. If this is why the calc preface was dropped I can understand to a degree. 

But, perhaps it can be given a new age rating of 18+ in the App Store?  Something that parents of children or teens can block their kids from downloading/exploiting if they set restrictions on various age ratings? 

Or, if already downloaded/in use by a minor, if the app switches its age rating to ‘18+ with restricted web access’, then this app would no longer function/would lock-out requiring parental approval/passcode to use again, that or prompting them to need to ask their parents for an upgrade to age restrictions in order to unlock. 

If the age rating was upgraded, then the checks/balances of parental settings would detect the app rating as violating parental restrictions & preventing use by minors to hide sensitive/inappropriate media from parents.  

Then you could bring back the calculator as a facade. 

Or maybe implement a more covert facade like a simple game with some kind of hidden access mechanism built into it like having to tap various corners of the screen in sequence to open the vault. If the preface were some kind of simple game it might make more sense that it takes up more space than a calculator ever should. 
 
Or perhaps a photo vault app with invisible lock screen in the background of the app, like those security screens that require a shape or easily remembered patterns to be traced on the screen before it unlocks the screen. 

Will we ever see the covert facades return?

This is perhaps the word app update I've ever experienced. If I could give it zero stars I would. I loved the old version(s) and have been using them for years. But now it seems as though Nike has become more enamored with the idea of making the app a social media platform and less interested in providing a user-friendly run tracking resource. Here are the problems I've experienced:
- Setting up the run is less intuitive. It used to be seamless and easy. 
- Distance calibration and pace calculation is suddenly terrible. It was off by over a 10th of a mile per mile this morning. 
- Related to the bullet above, it seems like it can't account for elevation changes like it used to. 
- Despite my running a tempo hill run, the app said my pace was the same from beginning to end. 
- Editing a run now requires saving the run, going back to activity, selecting the run, then pressing the Edit button. It used to be one click. 
- I'm currently running in Mizunos, and the app keeps returning to a default of a Nike shoe. 
- After editing a run you cannot see splits, a map, or anything other than the pace post-edit. 
- Music sync with the iPhone Music app is buggy. It stopped after one song, then played that song again, then when I tried to skip it, the Nike app played it again. 
- Post-run congrats now come from "your own personal Nike Running Coach." 

I've been using the Nike Running app for over five years and almost 4000 miles. I have loved it and owe it a debt of gratitude for giving me the motivation to start my life as a runner. However, my experience thus far with this update has been so negative that I'm thinking of dumping the app and seeking out another. Nike, please listen to the almost universal disdain for this update and fix your mistake, because right now, THIS APP IS TERRIBLE.

I love Pokemon, I've been playing the games since I was 11 and will probably continue to play them when I am old and decrepit. 
That being said, I am frustrated with this game for three reasons. 
Reason number one is the tracking glitch which makes is pretty much impossible to find nearby Pokemon. At first I figured that Niantic would be right in fixing this glitch and decided to wait for the next update, figuring a glitch that impacted gameplay as much as this one would be a high priority problem. Well, I was wrong, Niantic's most recent update was just to fix "minor text issues" and to make matters worse, Niantic doesn't even list this on their "known issues" support page. They won't even acknowledge the problem, and that is not good. 
My second issue is the server problems, including not being able to log on and the game freezing at inconvenient time, like when players activate lures/incense/lucky eggs, and the Shrodingers Pokemon issue. 
The third issue is a lack of information from Niantic to clarify where/when you can find certain types of Pokemon. 
This game has so much potential, but the way it is now is not as enjoyable as it could be. Step it up Niantic.
